subject: Mathematics
q: Let $f: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be a continuous function satisfying $f(0)=1$ and $f(2 \mathrm{x})-f(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{x}$ for all $\mathrm{x} \in \mathbb{R}$. If $\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}\left\{f(x)-f\left(\frac{x}{2^n}\right)\right\}=G(x)$, then $\sum_{r=1}^{10} G\left(r^2\right)$ is equal to
question: Let $f: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be a continuous function satisfying $f(0)=1$ and $f(2 \mathrm{x})-f(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{x}$ for all $\mathrm{x} \in \mathbb{R}$. If $\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}\left\{f(x)-f\left(\frac{x}{2^n}\right)\right\}=G(x)$, then $\sum_{r=1}^{10} G\left(r^2\right)$ is equal to
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& f(2 x)-f(x)=x \\ & f(x)-f\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)=\frac{x}{2} \\ & \mathrm{f}\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}}{2}\right)-\mathrm{f}\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}}{4}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{x}}{4} \\ & \mathrm{f}\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}}{4}\right)-\mathrm{f}\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}}{8}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{x}}{8} \\ & f\left(\frac{x}{2^{n-1}}\right)-f\left(\frac{x}{2^n}\right)=\frac{x}{2^n} \\ & f(2 x)-f\left(\frac{x}{2^n}\right)=x\left\{\frac{1-\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{n-1}}{1-\frac{1}{2}}\right\} \\ & f(x)-f\left(\frac{x}{2^n}\right)=2 x\left(1-\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{n+1}\right) \\ & \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})+\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{f}\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}}{2^{\mathrm{n}}}\right)=2 \mathrm{x}\left(1-\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\mathrm{n}+1}\right) \\ & \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}\left(f(x)-f\left(\frac{x}{2^n}\right)\right)=\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}\left(2 x\left(1-\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{n+1}\right)-x\right) \\ & G(x)=x \\ & \sum_{r=1}^{10} G\left(r^2\right)=\sum_{r=1}^{10} r^2=385
\end{aligned}$

q:
Let $f(x)=(x+1)^2-1, x \geq-1$
Statement-1: The set $\left\{x: f(x)=f^{-1}(x)\right\}=\{0,-1\}$
Statement-2 : $\mathrm{f}$ is a bijection.
question:

options: Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is true; Statement-2 is a correct explanation for Statement-1

Statement-1 is false, Statement-2 is true

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the range of the function $f(x)=6+16 \cos x \cdot \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3}-x\right) \cdot \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3}+x\right) \cdot \sin 3 x \cdot \cos 6 x, x \in \mathbf{R}$ be $[\alpha, \beta]$. Then the distance of the point $(\alpha, \beta)$ from the line $3 x+4 y+12=0$ is :
question: Let the range of the function $f(x)=6+16 \cos x \cdot \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3}-x\right) \cdot \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3}+x\right) \cdot \sin 3 x \cdot \cos 6 x, x \in \mathbf{R}$ be $[\alpha, \beta]$. Then the distance of the point $(\alpha, \beta)$ from the line $3 x+4 y+12=0$ is :
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& f(x)=6+16 \cos x \cdot \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3}-x\right) \\
& \qquad \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3}+x\right) \cdot \sin 3 x \cdot \cos 6 x \\
& f(x)=6+4 \cos 3 x \cdot \sin 3 x \cdot \cos 6 x \\
& \therefore \quad f(x)=6+\sin 12 x \\
& \therefore \quad \text { Range of } f(x)=[5,7] \\
& \therefore \quad[\alpha, \beta]=[5,7]
\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Distance of point from $3 x+4 y+12=0$
$\begin{aligned}
& =\left|\frac{3.5+4.7+12}{\sqrt{3^2+4^2}}\right| \\
& =11 \text { units }
\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: For a differentiable function $f: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$, suppose $f^{\prime}(x)=3 f(x)+\alpha$, where $\alpha \in \mathbb{R}$, $f(0)=1$ and $\lim _{x \rightarrow-\infty} f(x)=7$. Then $9 f\left(-\log _{\mathrm{e}} 3\right)$ is equal to_________
question: For a differentiable function $f: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$, suppose $f^{\prime}(x)=3 f(x)+\alpha$, where $\alpha \in \mathbb{R}$, $f(0)=1$ and $\lim _{x \rightarrow-\infty} f(x)=7$. Then $9 f\left(-\log _{\mathrm{e}} 3\right)$ is equal to_________
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{d y}{d x}-3 y=\alpha \\ & \text { If }=e^{\int-3 d x}=e^{-3 x} \\ & \therefore \quad y-e^{-3 x}=\int e^{-3 x} \cdot \alpha d x \\ & y e^{-3 x}=\frac{\alpha e^{-3 x}}{-3}+c \\ & \left(* e^{3 x}\right) \\ & y=\frac{\alpha}{-3}+C \cdot e^{3 x}\end{aligned}$
on substituting $\mathrm{x}=0, \mathrm{y}=1$
$x \rightarrow-\infty, y=7$
we get $y=7-6 e^{3 x}$
$9 f\left(-\log _e 3\right)=61$

q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi><mo>:</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>-</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mfrac><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mo>→</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>:</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>-</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mfrac><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mo>→</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> be defined as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi><mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mo>|</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>|</mo><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></mfrac></math>. Then the domain of the function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>fog</mi></math> is :
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi><mo>:</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>-</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mfrac><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mo>→</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>:</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>-</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mfrac><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mo>→</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> be defined as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi><mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mo>|</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>|</mo><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></mfrac></math>. Then the domain of the function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>fog</mi></math> is :
solution:
Given: gx=x+12x+5.

Also, gx≠-12

⇒x+12x+5≠-12

Case-I: x≥0

⇒gx=x+12x+5=-12

⇒2x+2=-2x-5

⇒4x=-7

⇒x=-74→Rejected as x≥0

Case-I: x<0

⇒-x+12x+5=-12

⇒-2x+2=-2x-5

⇒2=-5→Not possible

So, domain of fgx=domain of gx.

⇒Domain of fogx=R--52

q: The domain of the definition of the function <math><mi>f</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>-</mo><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfrac><mo>+</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is:
question: The domain of the definition of the function <math><mi>f</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>-</mo><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfrac><mo>+</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is:
solution:
We know that log10gx is defined when gx>0 and for a fraction to be defined its denominator must be non-zero.

Thus, the given function is defined only when

x3-x>0 and 4-x2≠0

⇒xx-1x+1>0 and x≠2, -2

The wavy curve for the inequality is

⇒x∈-1, 0∪1, ∞ and x≠2, -2.

Hence, domain is -1, 0∪1, 2∪2, ∞.

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of distinct real solutions of the equation $x|x+4|+3|x+2|+10=0$ is
question: The number of distinct real solutions of the equation $x|x+4|+3|x+2|+10=0$ is
solution:
Equation: $x|x+4| + 3|x+2| + 10 = 0$.
Critical points: $x = -4, -2$.
Case 1:
$x \ge -2$: $x(x+4) + 3(x+2) + 10 = 0$
$\Rightarrow x^2+7x+16=0$.
Discriminant $= 49-64 = -15 < 0$. No real roots.

Case 2:
$-4 \le x < -2$: $x(x+4) - 3(x+2) + 10 = 0$
$\Rightarrow x^2+x+4=0$.
Discriminant $= 1-16 = -15 < 0$. No real roots.

Case 3:
$x < -4$: $-x(x+4) - 3(x+2) + 10 = 0$
$\Rightarrow x^2+7x-4=0$.
$x = \frac{-7 \pm \sqrt{65}}{2}$.
$x = \frac{-7+\sqrt{65}}{2} \approx 0.53$ (rejected, not in $x<-4$). $x = \frac{-7-\sqrt{65}}{2} \approx -7.53$ (valid).

Number of distinct real solutions $= 1$.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $f$ be a function such that $3 f(x)+2 f\left(\frac{m}{19 x}\right)=5 x, x \neq 0$, where $m=\sum_{i=1}^{9}(i)^{2}$. Then $f(5)-f(2)$ is equal to
question: Let $f$ be a function such that $3 f(x)+2 f\left(\frac{m}{19 x}\right)=5 x, x \neq 0$, where $m=\sum_{i=1}^{9}(i)^{2}$. Then $f(5)-f(2)$ is equal to
solution:
$m = \sum_{i=1}^{9} i^2 = \frac{9 \cdot 10 \cdot 19}{6} = 285$, so $\frac{m}{19} = 15$.
The equation becomes $3f(x) + 2f\left(\frac{15}{x}\right) = 5x$ ...(i)
Replace $x$ by $\frac{15}{x}$: $3f\left(\frac{15}{x}\right) + 2f(x) = \frac{75}{x}$ ...(ii)
From $3 \times (i) - 2 \times (ii)$: $5f(x) = 15x - \frac{150}{x}$, so $f(x) = 3x - \frac{30}{x}$.
$f(5) = 15 - 6 = 9$, $f(2) = 6 - 15 = -9$.
$f(5) - f(2) = 9 - (-9) = 18$.

q: The number of bijective function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>⋯</mo><mo>,</mo><mn>99</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>→</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>⋯</mo><mo>,</mo><mn>100</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> if <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mn>3</mn></mfenced><mo>></mo><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mn>5</mn></mfenced><mo>></mo><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mn>7</mn></mfenced><mo>⋯</mo><mo>></mo><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mn>99</mn></mfenced></math> is
question: The number of bijective function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>⋯</mo><mo>,</mo><mn>99</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>→</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>⋯</mo><mo>,</mo><mn>100</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> if <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mn>3</mn></mfenced><mo>></mo><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mn>5</mn></mfenced><mo>></mo><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mn>7</mn></mfenced><mo>⋯</mo><mo>></mo><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mn>99</mn></mfenced></math> is
solution:
Given, f1,3,5,7,⋯,99→2,4,6,8,⋯,100

The number of elements in domain and codomain is 99.

Now, let us assume f1=100

Now as per diagram we have only 1 way for arranging f3>f5>f7⋯>f99,

Similarly if we choose f1=98 then again we have only 1 way for arranging  f3>f5>f7⋯>f99,

So we can see the arrangement f3>f5>f7⋯>f99 depends upon f1,

So number of ways for choosing f1 is C150

q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>{</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>}</mo></math>. Then the number of oneone functions <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mo>:</mo><mi>S</mi><mo>→</mo><mi>P</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>S</mi><mo>)</mo></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>S</mi><mo>)</mo></math> denote the power set of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math>, such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>n</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>⊂</mo><mi>f</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>m</mi><mo>)</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo><</mo><mi>m</mi></math> is
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>{</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>}</mo></math>. Then the number of oneone functions <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mo>:</mo><mi>S</mi><mo>→</mo><mi>P</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>S</mi><mo>)</mo></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>S</mi><mo>)</mo></math> denote the power set of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math>, such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>n</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>⊂</mo><mi>f</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>m</mi><mo>)</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo><</mo><mi>m</mi></math> is
solution:
Let S={1,2,3,4,5,6}, then the number of one-one functions, f:S·P(S), where P(S) denotes the power set of S, such that f(n)<f(m) where n<m is n(S)=6

PS=ϕ,{1},…{6},{1,2},…,{5,6},…,{1,2,3,4,5,6}

Case -1

f(5)= any 5 element subset A of S i.e. 6 choices

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $f:(1,\infty)\to\mathbb{R}$ be a function defined as $f(x) = \dfrac{x-1}{x+1}$. Let $f^{i+1}(x) = f(f^i(x))$, $i=1, 2, \ldots, 25$, where $f^1(x)=f(x)$. If $g(x) + f^{26}(x) = 0$, $x \in (1, \infty)$, then the area of the region bounded by the curves $y=g(x)$, $2y=2x-3$, $y=0$ and $x=4$ is:
question: Let $f:(1,\infty)\to\mathbb{R}$ be a function defined as $f(x) = \dfrac{x-1}{x+1}$. Let $f^{i+1}(x) = f(f^i(x))$, $i=1, 2, \ldots, 25$, where $f^1(x)=f(x)$. If $g(x) + f^{26}(x) = 0$, $x \in (1, \infty)$, then the area of the region bounded by the curves $y=g(x)$, $2y=2x-3$, $y=0$ and $x=4$ is:
solution:
Given $f(x) = \dfrac{x-1}{x+1}$. Let us find the first few compositions of $f(x)$:

$f^2(x) = f(f(x)) = \dfrac{\dfrac{x-1}{x+1}-1}{\dfrac{x-1}{x+1}+1} = \dfrac{x-1-x-1}{x-1+x+1} = \dfrac{-2}{2x} = -\dfrac{1}{x}$

$f^3(x) = f(f^2(x)) = f\left(-\dfrac{1}{x}\right) = \dfrac{-\dfrac{1}{x}-1}{-\dfrac{1}{x}+1} = \dfrac{-1-x}{-1+x} = \dfrac{x+1}{1-x}$

$f^4(x) = f(f^3(x)) = f\left(\dfrac{x+1}{1-x}\right) = \dfrac{\dfrac{x+1}{1-x}-1}{\dfrac{x+1}{1-x}+1} = \dfrac{x+1-1+x}{x+1+1-x} = \dfrac{2x}{2} = x$

Since $f^4(x) = x$, the sequence of functions is periodic with a period of $4$.

Therefore, $f^{26}(x) = f^{4 \times 6 + 2}(x) = f^2(x) = -\dfrac{1}{x}$.

We are given $g(x) + f^{26}(x) = 0$, which implies:
$g(x) - \dfrac{1}{x} = 0 \Rightarrow g(x) = \dfrac{1}{x}$

We need to find the area of the region bounded by the curves $y = \dfrac{1}{x}$, $y = x - \dfrac{3}{2}$ (from $2y = 2x - 3$), $y = 0$, and $x = 4$.

First, find the point of intersection of $y = \dfrac{1}{x}$ and $y = x - \dfrac{3}{2}$:
$\dfrac{1}{x} = x - \dfrac{3}{2} \Rightarrow 2x^2 - 3x - 2 = 0$
$(2x+1)(x-2) = 0$
Since $x \in (1, \infty)$, we get $x = 2$.

The line $y = x - \dfrac{3}{2}$ intersects the x-axis ($y = 0$) at $x = \dfrac{3}{2}$.

The required area $A$ is bounded by $y = x - \dfrac{3}{2}$ from $x = \dfrac{3}{2}$ to $x = 2$, and by $y = \dfrac{1}{x}$ from $x = 2$ to $x = 4$.

$A = \int_{3/2}^{2} \left(x - \dfrac{3}{2}\right) dx + \int_{2}^{4} \dfrac{1}{x} dx$

Evaluating the first integral:
$\int_{3/2}^{2} \left(x - \dfrac{3}{2}\right) dx = \left[ \dfrac{1}{2}\left(x - \dfrac{3}{2}\right)^2 \right]_{3/2}^{2} = \dfrac{1}{2} \left(2 - \dfrac{3}{2}\right)^2 - 0 = \dfrac{1}{2} \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^2 = \dfrac{1}{8}$

Evaluating the second integral:
$\int_{2}^{4} \dfrac{1}{x} dx = [\log_e x]_{2}^{4} = \log_e 4 - \log_e 2 = \log_e 2$

Total Area = $\dfrac{1}{8} + \log_e 2$

subject: Mathematics
q: If the domain of the function $f(x) = \sqrt{\log_{(0.6)}\left(\left|\dfrac{2x-5}{x^2-4}\right|\right)}$ is $(-\infty, a] \cup \{b\} \cup [c, d) \cup (e, \infty)$, then the value of $a + b + c + d + e$ is _______.
question: If the domain of the function $f(x) = \sqrt{\log_{(0.6)}\left(\left|\dfrac{2x-5}{x^2-4}\right|\right)}$ is $(-\infty, a] \cup \{b\} \cup [c, d) \cup (e, \infty)$, then the value of $a + b + c + d + e$ is _______.
solution:
For the function $f(x) = \sqrt{\log_{0.6}\left(\left|\dfrac{2x-5}{x^2-4} ight| ight)}$ to be defined, two conditions must be satisfied:

1. The argument of the logarithm must be strictly positive:
$\left|\dfrac{2x-5}{x^2-4} ight| > 0 \implies 2x - 5 eq 0 \implies x eq \dfrac{5}{2}$
Also, the denominator cannot be zero:
$x^2 - 4 eq 0 \implies x eq \pm 2$

2. The expression inside the square root must be non-negative:
$\log_{0.6}\left(\left|\dfrac{2x-5}{x^2-4} ight| ight) \ge 0$

Since the base of the logarithm is $0.6 < 1$, the inequality sign reverses when removing the logarithm:
$\left|\dfrac{2x-5}{x^2-4} ight| \le (0.6)^0$
$\left|\dfrac{2x-5}{x^2-4} ight| \le 1$

Since both sides are non-negative, we can square both sides:
$(2x-5)^2 \le (x^2-4)^2$
$(x^2-4)^2 - (2x-5)^2 \ge 0$

Using the identity $A^2 - B^2 = (A-B)(A+B)$:
$(x^2 - 4 - 2x + 5)(x^2 - 4 + 2x - 5) \ge 0$
$(x^2 - 2x + 1)(x^2 + 2x - 9) \ge 0$
$(x - 1)^2 (x^2 + 2x - 9) \ge 0$

Since $(x - 1)^2 \ge 0$ for all real $x$, the inequality holds if:
$x - 1 = 0 \implies x = 1$
or
$x^2 + 2x - 9 \ge 0$

Finding the roots of $x^2 + 2x - 9 = 0$ using the quadratic formula:
$x = \dfrac{-2 \pm \sqrt{4 - 4(1)(-9)}}{2} = \dfrac{-2 \pm \sqrt{40}}{2} = -1 \pm \sqrt{10}$

Thus, $x^2 + 2x - 9 \ge 0$ gives:
$x \in (-\infty, -1 - \sqrt{10}] \cup [-1 + \sqrt{10}, \infty)$

Combining this with $x = 1$, the solution to the inequality is:
$x \in (-\infty, -1 - \sqrt{10}] \cup \{1\} \cup [-1 + \sqrt{10}, \infty)$

Now, we must exclude the restricted values $x = \pm 2$ and $x = \dfrac{5}{2}$.
Note that $-1 - \sqrt{10} \approx -4.16$ and $-1 + \sqrt{10} \approx 2.16$.
The values $-2$ and $2$ do not fall in the above intervals, so they are already excluded.
However, $\dfrac{5}{2} = 2.5$, which lies in the interval $[-1 + \sqrt{10}, \infty)$. We must exclude it by splitting the interval:
$[-1 + \sqrt{10}, \dfrac{5}{2}) \cup (\dfrac{5}{2}, \infty)$

The final domain of the function is:
$(-\infty, -1 - \sqrt{10}] \cup \{1\} \cup [-1 + \sqrt{10}, \dfrac{5}{2}) \cup (\dfrac{5}{2}, \infty)$

Comparing this with the given domain $(-\infty, a] \cup \{b\} \cup [c, d) \cup (e, \infty)$, we get:
$a = -1 - \sqrt{10}$
$b = 1$
$c = -1 + \sqrt{10}$
$d = \dfrac{5}{2}$
$e = \dfrac{5}{2}$

We need to find the value of $a + b + c + d + e$:
$a + b + c + d + e = (-1 - \sqrt{10}) + 1 + (-1 + \sqrt{10}) + \dfrac{5}{2} + \dfrac{5}{2}$
$= -1 - \sqrt{10} + 1 - 1 + \sqrt{10} + 5$
$= 4$

Answer: $4$

q:
Let a function $f:(0, \infty) \rightarrow(0, \infty)$ be defined by

$f(x)=\left|1-\frac{1}{x}\right| .$ Then $f$ is :
question:
Let a function $f:(0, \infty) \rightarrow(0, \infty)$ be defined by

$f(x)=\left|1-\frac{1}{x}\right| .$ Then $f$ is :
options: not injective but it is surjective
solution:
$f:(0, \infty) \rightarrow(0, \infty)$

$f(x)=\left|1-\frac{1}{x}\right|$ is not a function

$\because f(1)=0$ and $1 \in$ domain but $0 \notin$ co-domain

Hence, $f(x)$ is not a function.

question: The real valued function fx=cosec-1xx-[x], where x denotes the greatest integer less than or equal to x, is defined for all x belonging to:

q: If fx=4x+36x-4, x≠23 and (fof) (x)=g(x), where g:R-23→R-23, then (gogog) (4) is equal to
question: If fx=4x+36x-4, x≠23 and (fof) (x)=g(x), where g:R-23→R-23, then (gogog) (4) is equal to
solution:
Given: fx=4x+36x-4

⇒fofx=44x+36x-4+364x+36x-4-4

⇒fofx=16x+12+18x-1224x+18-24x+16

⇒fofx=34x34

⇒fofx=x

⇒gx=x

⇒gogx=x

⇒gogogx=x

⇒gogog4=4

q: Let $f:(-1,1) \rightarrow B$, be a function defined by $f(x)=\tan ^{-1} \frac{2 x}{1-x^2}$, then $f$ is both one-one and onto when $B$ is the interval
question: Let $f:(-1,1) \rightarrow B$, be a function defined by $f(x)=\tan ^{-1} \frac{2 x}{1-x^2}$, then $f$ is both one-one and onto when $B$ is the interval
solution:
Given $f(x)=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 x}{1-x^2}\right)$ for $x \in(-1,1)$
clearly range of $f(x)=\left(-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$.
$\therefore$ co-domain of function $=B=\left(-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$.

fx=y=x-2x-3

∴f-1x=3x-2x-1

∴ x2-5x+6=0 has roots x1,x2

q: If $f(x)=\frac{2^x}{2^x+\sqrt{2}}, \mathrm{x} \in \mathbb{R}$, then $\sum_{\mathrm{k}=1}^{81} f\left(\frac{\mathrm{k}}{82}\right)$ is equal to
question: If $f(x)=\frac{2^x}{2^x+\sqrt{2}}, \mathrm{x} \in \mathbb{R}$, then $\sum_{\mathrm{k}=1}^{81} f\left(\frac{\mathrm{k}}{82}\right)$ is equal to
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & f(x)=\frac{2^x}{2^x+\sqrt{2}} \\ & f(x)+f(1-x)=\frac{2^x}{2^x+\sqrt{2}}+\frac{2^{1-x}}{2^{1-x}+\sqrt{2}} \\ & =\frac{2^x}{2^x+\sqrt{2}}+\frac{2}{2+\sqrt{2} 2^x}=\frac{2^x+\sqrt{2}}{2^x+\sqrt{2}}=1\end{aligned}$


$\begin{aligned} & \text { Now, } \sum_{\mathrm{k}=1}^{81} \mathrm{f}\left(\frac{\mathrm{k}}{82}\right)=\mathrm{f}\left(\frac{1}{82}\right)+\mathrm{f}\left(\frac{2}{82}\right)+\ldots \ldots+\mathrm{f}\left(\frac{81}{82}\right) \\ & =\mathrm{f}\left(\frac{1}{82}\right)+\mathrm{f}\left(\frac{1}{82}\right)+\ldots \ldots+\mathrm{f}\left(1-\frac{2}{82}\right)+\mathrm{f}\left(1-\frac{1}{82}\right)\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & {\left[\mathrm{f}\left(\frac{1}{82}\right)+\mathrm{f}\left(1-\frac{1}{82}\right)\right]+\left[\mathrm{f}\left(\frac{2}{82}\right)+\mathrm{f}\left(1-\frac{2}{82}\right)\right]+\ldots .40 \text { cases }+\mathrm{f}\left(\frac{41}{82}\right)} \\ & (1+1+\ldots .+1) 40 \text { times }+\frac{2^{1 / 2}}{2^{1 / 2}+2^{1 / 2}} \\ & 40+\frac{1}{2}=\frac{81}{2}\end{aligned}$

q: Let $f(x)=\log _{\mathrm{e}} x$ and $g(x)=\frac{x^4-2 x^3+3 x^2-2 x+2}{2 x^2-2 x+1}$. Then the domain of $f \circ g$ is
question: Let $f(x)=\log _{\mathrm{e}} x$ and $g(x)=\frac{x^4-2 x^3+3 x^2-2 x+2}{2 x^2-2 x+1}$. Then the domain of $f \circ g$ is
solution:
$f(g(x))=\ln \left(\frac{x^4-2 x^3+3 x^2-2 x+2}{2 x^2-2 x+1}\right)$

Since $2 x^2-2 x+1>0 \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R} \because(-2)^2-4(2) < 0$
Consider
$\begin{aligned}
& x^4-2 x^3+3 x^2-2 x+2 \\
& =\left(x^4-2 x^3+x^2\right)+\left(x^2-2 x+1\right)+\left(1+x^2\right) \\
& =x^2(x-1)^2+(x-1)^2+\left(x^2+1\right)>0 \forall x \in \mathbb{R} \\
& \Rightarrow g(x)>0 \forall x \in \mathbb{R} \\
& \Rightarrow \ln f((x)), f(x)>0 \forall x \in \mathbb{R} \\
& \Rightarrow x \in \mathbb{R} \text { is domain }
\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $[x]$ denote the greatest integer less than or equal to $x$. Then the domain of $f(x)=\sec ^{-1}(2[x]+1)$ is :
question: Let $[x]$ denote the greatest integer less than or equal to $x$. Then the domain of $f(x)=\sec ^{-1}(2[x]+1)$ is :
solution:
$\begin{array}{ll}
f(x)=\sec ^{-1}(2[x]+1) \\
\Rightarrow 2[x]+1 \geq 1 & \text { or } 2[x]+1 \leq-1 \\
\Rightarrow 2[x] \geq 0 & \text { or } 2[x] \leq-2 \\
\Rightarrow[x] \geq 0 & \text { or }[x] \leq-1 \\
\Rightarrow x \geq 0 & \text { or } x \leq 0
\end{array}$
Domain of $f(x)$ is $(-\infty, \infty)$

q: The range of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfrac></mfenced></math> is
question: The range of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfrac></mfenced></math> is
solution:
Given,

fx=4sin-1x2x2+1

Now taking, x21+x2=1-11+x2<1

⇒0≤x21+x2<1

Now taking sin-1 we get,

⇒0≤sin-1x21+x2<π2

⇒0≤4sin-1x21+x2<2π

Hence, the range of 4sin-1x2x2+1 is [0,2π)

subject: Mathematics
q:
If the domain of the function
$f(x)=\log _e\left(\frac{2 x-3}{5+4 x}\right)+\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{4+3 x}{2-x}\right) \quad \text { is } \quad[\alpha, \beta)$
then $\alpha^2+4 \beta$ is equal to
question:
If the domain of the function
$f(x)=\log _e\left(\frac{2 x-3}{5+4 x}\right)+\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{4+3 x}{2-x}\right) \quad \text { is } \quad[\alpha, \beta)$
then $\alpha^2+4 \beta$ is equal to
solution:
Given function is
$f(x)=\log _e\left(\frac{2 x-3}{5+4 x}\right)+\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{4+3 x}{2-x}\right)$
For domain, the conditions are
$\frac{2 x-3}{5+4 x} \gt 0 \text { and }\left|\frac{4+3 x}{2-x}\right| \leq 1$
Now, $\frac{2 \mathrm{x}-3}{5+4 \mathrm{x}} \gt 0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \in\left(-\infty,-\frac{5}{4}\right) \cup\left[\frac{3}{2}, \infty\right)$
and $\quad-1 \leq \frac{4+3 x}{2-x} \leq 1$
$\Rightarrow\left(-1 \leq \frac{4+3 x}{2-x}\right) \cap\left(\frac{4+3 x}{2-x} \leq 1\right)$
$\Rightarrow\left(\frac{6+2 \mathrm{x}}{2-\mathrm{x}} \geq 0\right) \cap\left(\frac{2+4 \mathrm{x}}{2-\mathrm{x}} \leq 0\right)$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{6+2 x}{2-x} \cdot \frac{2+4 x}{2-x} \leq 0 \\ & \Rightarrow x \in\left[-3,-\frac{1}{2}\right]\end{aligned}$
Hence, we get the domain of f as $\mathrm{x} \in\left[-3,-\frac{5}{4}\right)$ This means that $\alpha=-3, \beta=-\frac{5}{4}$ Thus, $\alpha^2+4 \beta=9-5=4$

subject: Mathematics
q: For <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>-</mo><mfenced open="{" close="}" separators="|"><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>f</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>f</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>f</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfrac></math> be three given functions. If a function, <math><mi>J</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> satisfies <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mi>f</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi>o</mi><mi>J</mi><mi>o</mi><msub><mi>f</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mfenced separators="|"><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>f</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mi>x</mi></mfenced></math> then <math><mi>J</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to:
question: For <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>-</mo><mfenced open="{" close="}" separators="|"><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>f</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>f</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>f</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfrac></math> be three given functions. If a function, <math><mi>J</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> satisfies <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mi>f</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi>o</mi><mi>J</mi><mi>o</mi><msub><mi>f</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mfenced separators="|"><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>f</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mi>x</mi></mfenced></math> then <math><mi>J</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to:
solution:
Given f2Jf1x=f3x

⇒f2J1x=11-x

⇒1-J1x=11-x

⇒J1x=1-11-x=-x1-x

⇒Jx=-1x1-1x

=-1x-1

=11-x

∴ Jx=f3x

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mo>:</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">ℝ</mi><mo>→</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">ℝ</mi></math> be a function defined by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>log</mi><msqrt><mi>m</mi></msqrt></msub><mo> </mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mfenced><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>cos</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mi>m</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>, for some <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math>, such that the range of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>. Then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is _____ .
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mo>:</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">ℝ</mi><mo>→</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">ℝ</mi></math> be a function defined by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>log</mi><msqrt><mi>m</mi></msqrt></msub><mo> </mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mfenced><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>cos</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mi>m</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>, for some <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math>, such that the range of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>. Then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is _____ .
solution:
Given,

f:ℝ→ℝ be a function defined by fx=logm 2sinx-cosx+m-2, for some m,

Also given the range of f is 0,2,

Now we know that,

-2≤sinx-cosx≤2

⇒-2≤2sinx-cosx≤2

(Assuming 2sinx-cosx=k)

⇒-2≤k≤2        …1

Now taking function fx=logmk+m-2

Given, 0≤fx≤2

⇒0≤logmk+m-2≤2

⇒1≤k+m-2≤m

⇒-m+3≤k≤2         …2

Now from equations 1 & 2, we get

-m+3=-2

⇒m=5
